name: Gilillo Peak Circular Route from Cazorla
name_original: Nacimiento del Guadalquivir desde Cazorla - circular por el P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas
description: This difficult 25.9-mile mountain biking route explores the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park to the Guadalquivir River s
region_intro: Mountain biking around Cazorla offers diverse and rugged landscapes within the Natural Park of Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas. The region features extensive pine forests, steep mountains, and dramatic river gorges, providing varied terrain for riders. Elevation changes are significant, with routes traversing from valley floors to high mountain passes. This area is characterized by its karstic landscapes and the sources of major rivers, creating a challenging and scenic environment for mountain biking. Best mountain bike trails around Cazorla The most popular mountain bike trail is Gilillo Peak Circular Route from Cazorla, a 25.9 miles (41.6 km) trail that takes 4 hours 29 minutes to complete, featuring substantial elevation gain to a prominent peak. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Cazorla – Prepared pine loop from Cazorla, a difficult 48.8 miles (78.6 km) path. This route navigates through dense pine forests and offers extensive views of the natural park. Local mountain bikers also love the Collado Zamora Refuge – Cazorla loop from Cazorla, a 29.9 miles (48.1 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain and past a refuge, often completed in about 5 hours 3 minutes. Mountain biking around Cazorla is defined by rugged terrain, extensive pine forests, and dramatic river gorges. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels, catering to experienced riders. The routes in Cazorla are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 20 reviews. More than 600 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Cazorla's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available around Cazorla?
A: Cazorla offers a robust selection of over 20 mountain bike routes, catering primarily to experienced riders. The network includes 6 moderate and 14 difficult trails, ensuring a challenging and rewarding experience for those seeking adventure in the rugged landscapes of the Natural Park of Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when mountain biking in Cazorla?
A: Mountain biking in Cazorla features diverse and rugged terrain. You'll encounter steep mountains, extensive pine forests, and dramatic river gorges. The landscape is characterized by karstic formations, with surfaces ranging from soil to rocky paths, often involving significant elevation changes as routes traverse from valley floors to high mountain passes.

Q: Are there any family-friendly mountain bike trails in Cazorla?
A: The mountain bike trails around Cazorla are generally geared towards experienced riders, with routes classified as moderate or difficult. There are no easy trails listed in this selection, suggesting that routes may be too challenging for young children or beginners. For family outings, consider exploring less technical paths or designated cycling routes within the villages like Cazorla, La Iruela, or Arroyo Frío, which might offer gentler terrain.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I might see on a mountain bike ride in Cazorla?
A: Cazorla is rich in natural beauty. You can expect to see extensive pine forests, towering peaks, and dramatic river features. The region is home to the sources of the Guadalquivir and Segura rivers, and you might encounter stunning gorges like Cerrada de Utrero or Elías Gorge. Waterfalls such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4473525" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Malena Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; are also present, adding to the scenic appeal.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in Cazorla?
A: Yes, several circular mountain bike routes are available. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990342514/gilillo-peak-circular-route-from-cazorla" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gilillo Peak Circular Route from Cazorla&lt;/a&gt; is a 25.9-mile (41.6 km) difficult trail with substantial elevation gain. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123886" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado Zamora Refuge – Cazorla loop from Cazorla&lt;/a&gt;, a 29.9-mile (48.1 km) difficult trail passing a mountain refuge.

Q: What do other mountain bikers say about the trails in Cazorla?
A: The mountain bike routes in Cazorla are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 600 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore the varied terrain, often praising the challenging nature of the trails and the spectacular landscapes of the natural park.

Q: Are there any mountain bike routes that pass by mountain refuges or huts?
A: Yes, some routes in Cazorla pass by mountain refuges. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123886" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado Zamora Refuge – Cazorla loop from Cazorla&lt;/a&gt; leads through mountainous terrain and past the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3229805" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado Zamora Refuge&lt;/a&gt;. Other refuges in the area include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3980302" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente de Acero Refuge&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4360971" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Sacejo Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, which can serve as points of interest or rest stops.

Q: What is the best season for mountain biking in Cazorla?
A: Cazorla offers good mountain biking conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide the most pleasant temperatures for riding. Summers can be hot, especially at lower elevations, so early mornings or late afternoons are preferable. Winters can bring colder temperatures and occasional snow at higher altitudes, but many trails remain accessible.

Q: Are there any routes with significant elevation gain for advanced riders?
A: Absolutely. Cazorla is known for its challenging routes with substantial elevation changes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38168373" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazorla – Prepared pine loop from Cazorla&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 48.8-mile (78.6 km) path with over 2,400 meters of elevation gain, navigating through dense pine forests. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990342514/gilillo-peak-circular-route-from-cazorla" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gilillo Peak Circular Route from Cazorla&lt;/a&gt; also features significant climbing to a prominent peak.

Q: Can I find routes that offer extensive views of the natural park?
A: Many routes in Cazorla provide extensive views of the Natural Park of Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38168373" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazorla – Prepared pine loop from Cazorla&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, offers wide vistas across the park's dense pine forests. Additionally, routes ascending to peaks or viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31865809" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Palomas Viewpoint – Cazorla loop from Cazorla&lt;/a&gt; are designed to showcase the region's dramatic mountainous scenery.

Q: Are there any historical sites or charming villages accessible by mountain bike in the Cazorla area?
A: While the mountain bike trails themselves focus on natural landscapes, the region surrounding Cazorla is dotted with charming villages and historical sites. The village of Cazorla itself, with its narrow streets and La Yedra Castle, serves as a primary entry point. Other notable villages like La Iruela and Hornos de Segura also boast castles and historical charm, offering cultural stops if you venture off the main trails.

This municipality in the province of Jaén, in Andalusia, is located on the slopes of the Sierra de Cazorla, crowning the Guadalquivir valley. One of its most emblematic monuments is its castle, dating from the 12th century and located at the top of a hill.
The incredibly pretty town of Carzola is well worth of visit, with historic buildings constructed on the hillside.

faqs.question: What makes this mountain bike route challenging?
faqs.answer: This route is considered difficult due to its significant accumulated elevation gain and diverse terrain. You'll encounter long stretches of forest tracks, some asphalted sections, and parts that are sandy and rocky. There are also steep and deteriorated areas, and some segments are practically un-bikable, requiring you to push your bike unless you are highly skilled. It demands good physical fitness and advanced riding skills.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers breathtaking and varied landscapes. You'll get panoramic views of the historic town of Cazorla and its castle, wind through impressive karstic mountains and immense pine forests, and see vast olive groves in the valleys. Key scenic points include the tranquil Source of the Guadalquivir River, the impressive rock faces of El Chorro (where griffon vultures can sometimes be seen), and Collado Zamora, which offers expansive views of Sierra Mágina and Sierra Nevada.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: While the route can be started from various points, the town of La Iruela is a common and recommended starting point. You can often find parking within or near the town to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Is this mountain bike trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are allowed in the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, but they must be kept on a leash at all times to protect wildlife and ensure their safety, especially given the challenging terrain and potential for encounters with other park users.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to ride this trail?
faqs.answer: The best seasons for mountain biking this route are spring and autumn. During these times, the weather is typically milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter conditions of winter. Spring also offers lush greenery and blooming flora.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for the Natural Park?
faqs.answer: No, there are no general entrance fees to access the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. For typical mountain biking activities on established trails like this one, specific permits are generally not required. However, always check local regulations for any updated information or specific restrictions, especially if planning off-trail activities or large group events.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other trails within the park. You'll ride parts of the Andalucía - North Route (Província de Jaén), Cortados del Chorro, Picón del Rey - Arroyo de los Cierzos, El Gilillo, and Puerto del Tejo - Arroyo de los Cierzos, among others. This allows for a diverse riding experience through different parts of the natural park.

faqs.question: What are the key points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: Beyond the stunning natural landscapes, you'll encounter several notable points. These include the Source of the Guadalquivir River, the historic Castillo de La Iruela, and the town of Cazorla. You'll also pass by the legendary Puente de las Herrerías and the Collado Zamora Refuge, which offers fantastic views.

faqs.question: What essential gear should I bring for this mountain bike trail?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's difficulty and length, essential gear includes a well-maintained mountain bike suitable for varied terrain, a helmet, appropriate cycling apparel, plenty of water and snacks, a repair kit (including spare tubes/patch kit), a first-aid kit, and navigation tools (GPS device or smartphone with offline maps). Sun protection and layers of clothing are also recommended.

faqs.question: Are there any sections where I might need to push my bike?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail research indicates that some sections involve very complicated descents and tough ascents, along with potentially 'inciclable' (un-bikable) segments. Riders should be prepared to dismount and push their bikes through these parts, especially if they are not highly skilled in technical mountain biking.

faqs.question: Is there an 'insider tip' for riding this circular route?
faqs.answer: While the route is circular and can be started from various points, beginning in La Iruela is a popular choice. Consider riding the route in a clockwise direction if you prefer to tackle some of the steeper ascents earlier in your ride, potentially making the latter half more manageable. Always check the latest trail conditions before you go, as water erosion can significantly impact certain sections.
